annotations.label: Set

anchor.snippet: a set is a collection of different things

annotations.note: `Set α := α → Prop` defines a set as a collection of elements of type `α`.

annotations.label: Element / membership

anchor.snippet: is an element of a set

annotations.note: `Set.Mem` together with the `Membership α (Set α)` instance gives the `a ∈ s` predicate.

annotations.label: Axiom of extensionality

anchor.snippet: The axiom of extensionality states that two sets are equal if and only if they have the same elements

annotations.note: `Set.ext` proves equality of sets from `∀ x, x ∈ a ↔ x ∈ b`.

annotations.label: Empty set

anchor.snippet: It is called the empty set

annotations.note: The `EmptyCollection (Set α)` instance defines `∅ : Set α` as `fun _ ↦ False`.

annotations.label: Singleton

anchor.snippet: A singleton is a set with exactly one element

annotations.note: `Set.singleton a = {b | b = a}` with `Singleton α (Set α)` instance giving `{a}` notation.

annotations.label: Finite set

anchor.snippet: A set is finite if there exists a natural number

annotations.note: `Set.Finite s` expresses that `s : Set α` is a finite subset.

annotations.label: Infinite set

anchor.snippet: A set is infinite if such an

annotations.note: `Set.Infinite s` is defined as `¬ s.Finite`.

annotations.label: Natural numbers form an infinite set

anchor.snippet: The natural numbers form an infinite set

annotations.note: Mathlib provides an `Infinite ℕ` instance, equivalent to `(Set.univ : Set ℕ).Infinite`.

annotations.label: Roster notation

anchor.snippet: Roster or enumeration notation is a notation introduced by Ernst Zermelo

annotations.note: Mathlib supports `{a, b, c}` via the `Insert`/`Singleton` instances rather than a distinct "roster notation" definition.

annotations.label: Set-builder notation

anchor.snippet: Set-builder notation specifies a set as being the set of all elements that satisfy some logical formula

annotations.note: The `{x | p x}` syntax is implemented by `setOf` together with the `setBuilder` term elaborator.

anchors.snippet: Set-builder notation specifies a set as being the set of all elements that satisfy some logical formula

anchors.value: {\displaystyle F=\{n\mid n{\text{ is an integer, and }}0\leq n\leq 19\}.}

annotations.label: Subset

anchor.snippet: A subset of a set

annotations.note: `Set.Subset s₁ s₂ := ∀ ⦃a⦄, a ∈ s₁ → a ∈ s₂` provides the `⊆` relation.

annotations.label: Proper subset

anchor.snippet: is a proper subset of a set

annotations.note: Proper subset `⊂` on `Set α` is provided via the `HasSSubset` instance derived from the partial order.

annotations.label: Humans are a proper subset of mammals

anchor.snippet: The set of all humans is a proper subset of the set of all mammals

annotations.note: Informal real-world illustration with no Mathlib analogue.

annotations.label: Equality via mutual containment

anchor.snippet: Two sets are equal if and only if they contain each other

annotations.note: `Set.Subset.antisymm_iff` gives `a = b ↔ a ⊆ b ∧ b ⊆ a`.

annotations.label: Empty set is a subset of every set

anchor.snippet: The empty set is a subset of every set

annotations.note: `Set.empty_subset (s : Set α) : ∅ ⊆ s`.

annotations.label: Intersection of two sets

anchor.snippet: The intersection of two sets

annotations.note: `Set.inter s₁ s₂ = {a | a ∈ s₁ ∧ a ∈ s₂}`, providing the `∩` notation via `Inter (Set α)`.

anchors.value: {\displaystyle A\cap B=\{x\mid x\in A\land x\in B\},}

annotations.label: Intersection is associative and commutative

anchor.snippet: Intersection is associative and commutative

annotations.note: `Set.inter_assoc` and `Set.inter_comm` formalize associativity and commutativity of intersection.

annotations.label: Intersection of a family of sets

anchor.snippet: is a nonempty set of sets, its intersection

annotations.note: `Set.sInter` (`⋂₀`) and `Set.iInter` (`⋂ i, …`) define arbitrary intersections.

anchors.snippet: is a nonempty set of sets, its intersection

annotations.label: Union of two sets

anchor.snippet: The union of two sets

annotations.note: `Set.union s₁ s₂ = {a | a ∈ s₁ ∨ a ∈ s₂}`, providing `∪` via `Union (Set α)`.

anchors.value: {\displaystyle A\cup B=\{x\mid x\in A\lor x\in B\},}

annotations.label: Union is associative and commutative

anchor.snippet: Union is associative and commutative

annotations.note: `Set.union_assoc` and `Set.union_comm` formalize associativity and commutativity of union.

annotations.label: Union of a family of sets

anchor.snippet: is a set of sets, its union

annotations.note: `Set.sUnion` (`⋃₀`) and `Set.iUnion` define arbitrary unions.

anchors.snippet: is a set of sets, its union

annotations.label: Set difference

anchor.snippet: The set difference of two sets

annotations.note: `Set.diff s t = {a ∈ s | a ∉ t}` providing `\` via the `SDiff` instance.

anchors.snippet: The set difference of two sets

anchors.value: {\displaystyle A\setminus B=\{x\mid x\in A\land x\not \in B\},}

annotations.label: Complement / absolute complement

anchor.snippet: the difference

annotations.note: `Set.compl s = {a | a ∉ s}` provides `sᶜ`.

annotations.label: Symmetric difference

anchor.snippet: The symmetric difference of two sets

annotations.note: `symmDiff` on any `GeneralizedBooleanAlgebra` specializes to symmetric difference on `Set α`.

anchors.snippet: The symmetric difference of two sets

annotations.label: Powerset

anchor.snippet: The set of all subsets of a set

annotations.note: `Set.powerset s = {t | t ⊆ s}` with notation `𝒫 s`.

annotations.label: Powerset is a Boolean ring

anchor.snippet: The powerset is a Boolean ring that has symmetric difference as addition

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `AsBoolRing` converting any `BooleanAlgebra` (including `Set α`) to a `BooleanRing`, but no direct `BooleanRing (Set α)` instance.

annotations.label: Powerset is a Boolean algebra

anchor.snippet: The powerset is also a Boolean algebra

annotations.note: `Set α` is endowed with a `BooleanAlgebra` instance.

annotations.label: Powerset is a complete lattice

anchor.snippet: the powerset is also a partially ordered set for set inclusion

annotations.note: `Set α` carries a `CompleteAtomicBooleanAlgebra` instance, which in particular makes it a complete lattice.

annotations.label: Function

anchor.snippet: is a rule that assigns to each element

annotations.note: Functions `α → β` are a primitive notion in Lean's type theory used throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: Value, image, domain, codomain

anchor.snippet: it is called the value of

annotations.note: Domain/codomain are intrinsic to the function type `α → β`, and `Set.image f s = {f a | a ∈ s}` provides the image.

annotations.label: Graph of a function

anchor.snippet: The graph of a function

annotations.note: `Function.graph f` defines the graph `{(x, f x) | x}` of `f : α → β`.

annotations.label: Formal definition of function as a triple

anchor.snippet: This point of view allows one to formally define

annotations.note: Mathlib uses Lean's intrinsic function type rather than encoding functions as (domain, codomain, graph) triples.

annotations.label: Indexed family

anchor.snippet: an indexed family is a function that has the index set as its domain

annotations.note: Indexed families are represented as ordinary functions `ι → X` in Lean; no separate definition is needed.

annotations.label: Ordered pair, tuple, sequence as indexed families

anchor.snippet: an indexed family is called an ordered pair

annotations.note: Lean provides `Prod`, `Tuple/Fin n → α`, and sequences `ℕ → α` directly, but not as instances of one shared "indexed family" definition.

annotations.label: Cartesian product of two sets

anchor.snippet: their Cartesian product

annotations.note: `Set.prod s t = s ×ˢ t : Set (α × β)`.

anchors.value: {\displaystyle A\times B=\{(a,b)\mid a\in A{\text{ and }}b\in B\}.}

annotations.label: Cartesian product of an indexed family

anchor.snippet: Given any indexed family of sets

annotations.note: `Set.pi s t` defines the dependent product of an indexed family of sets.

annotations.label: Product of nonempty sets is nonempty

anchor.snippet: The axiom of choice implies that any product of nonempty sets is nonempty

annotations.note: Lean's `Classical.axiomOfChoice` is exactly the statement that a Π-type of nonempty types is nonempty.

annotations.label: Set exponentiation

anchor.snippet: the set exponentiation

annotations.note: `E^F` is represented by the function type `F → E` in Lean.

annotations.label: Power set

anchor.snippet: The power set of a set

annotations.note: Same as above: `Set.powerset s` with notation `𝒫 s`.

anchors.snippet: The power set of a set

annotations.label: Bijection between subsets and indicator functions

anchor.snippet: There is a natural one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Set.indicator` and `mk_set : #(Set α) = 2 ^ #α`, expressing the bijection cardinally, but no single `Equiv (Set α) (α → Bool)` declaration that I located.

anchors.snippet: There is a natural one-to-one correspondence

annotations.label: Cardinality of the power set

anchor.snippet: In particular, if

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk_powerset : #(↥(𝒫 s)) = 2 ^ #(↥s)`.

annotations.label: Disjoint union of two sets

anchor.snippet: The disjoint union of two sets

annotations.note: The disjoint union of two types is the `Sum` type `α ⊕ β`.

anchors.snippet: The disjoint union of two sets

anchors.value: {\displaystyle A\sqcup B=\{(a,i)\mid (i=1\land a\in A)\lor (i=2\land a\in B\}.}

annotations.label: Disjoint union of an indexed family

anchor.snippet: The disjoint union of two sets is a particular case of the disjoint union of an indexed family

annotations.note: The disjoint union of an indexed family is the sigma type `Σ i, X i`.

anchors.snippet: The disjoint union of two sets is a particular case of the disjoint union of an indexed family

annotations.label: Disjoint union is the coproduct in Set

anchor.snippet: The disjoint union is the coproduct in the category of sets

annotations.note: Category theory in Mathlib has coproduct constructions in `Type`/`Set`, but I did not locate a single named theorem matching the article's wording.

anchors.snippet: The disjoint union is the coproduct in the category of sets

annotations.label: Forgetting-indices map is bijective iff pairwise disjoint

anchor.snippet: This map is always surjective

annotations.note: Lemmas like the sigma-to-iUnion projection being injective under pairwise disjointness exist (e.g. via `Set.PairwiseDisjoint`), but I could not locate a single theorem stating the equivalence verbatim.

annotations.label: Partition

anchor.snippet: If a set is the disjoint union of a family of subsets

annotations.note: `Setoid.IsPartition` formalizes a partition of a set into nonempty disjoint blocks.

annotations.label: Cardinality

anchor.snippet: the cardinality of a set

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk α` (notation `#α`) is the cardinality of `α`, with `#s` for `s : Set α` via the subtype.

annotations.label: Same cardinality

anchor.snippet: have the same cardinality if there exists a one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq : #α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` formalizes equinumerosity.

annotations.label: Naturals and even naturals have same cardinality

anchor.snippet: the natural numbers and the even natural numbers have the same cardinality

annotations.note: I did not locate a named lemma stating that `ℕ` and the even naturals are equinumerous, although it is trivially provable from existing API.

annotations.label: Characterization of infinite sets

anchor.snippet: a set is infinite if and only if it has the same cardinality as one of its proper subsets

annotations.note: Mathlib has both `Set.Infinite` and Dedekind-style results (e.g. `Infinite.exists_subset_card_eq`), but I did not locate a single `infinite_iff` matching this Dedekind characterization.

annotations.label: Cardinality inequality

anchor.snippet: has a cardinality smaller than or equal to the cardinality of another set

annotations.note: `≤` on `Cardinal` is defined via existence of an injection between representatives.

annotations.label: Schröder–Bernstein theorem

anchor.snippet: Schröder–Bernstein theorem implies

annotations.note: `Function.Embedding.schroeder_bernstein` gives a bijection from mutually injective maps.

annotations.label: Cardinality inequality is a total order

anchor.snippet: inequality of cardinalities is a total order

annotations.note: `Cardinal.linearOrder` is the `LinearOrder Cardinal` instance, proved using AC.

annotations.label: Countable and uncountable sets

anchor.snippet: are called countable sets

annotations.note: `Set.Countable s` and the negation `¬ s.Countable` formalize countable/uncountable.

annotations.label: Cantor's diagonal argument

anchor.snippet: Cantor's diagonal argument shows that

annotations.note: `Function.cantor_surjective` (and `Cardinal.cantor : a < 2 ^ a`) formalize Cantor's diagonal argument.

annotations.label: Cardinality of the continuum

anchor.snippet: The cardinality of set of the real numbers is called the cardinality of the continuum

annotations.note: `Cardinal.continuum := 2 ^ ℵ₀`, with notation `𝔠`.

annotations.label: Subsets containing an open interval have cardinality c

anchor.snippet: every subset of

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `Cardinal.mk_Ioo_real : #(Ioo a b) = 𝔠` and `mk_set_le`, from which the article's statement follows but is not packaged as a single theorem.

annotations.label: Cardinality of reals equals power set of naturals

anchor.snippet: meaning that the cardinality of the real numbers equals the cardinality of the power set of the natural numbers

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk_real : #ℝ = 𝔠 = 2 ^ ℵ₀`, equivalently `#(Set ℕ)`.

anchors.snippet: meaning that the cardinality of the real numbers equals the cardinality of the power set of the natural numbers

annotations.label: Cardinality of Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: is also the cardinality of the entire plane

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk_complex : #ℂ = 𝔠` formalizes that the plane has cardinality continuum.

annotations.label: Continuum hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The continuum hypothesis

annotations.note: I found no `ContinuumHypothesis` or analogous statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cohen's independence of CH

anchor.snippet: Paul Cohen proved that the continuum hypothesis is independent

annotations.note: Independence of CH is a metatheorem about ZFC and is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axiom of choice

anchor.snippet: A more formal statement of the axiom of choice

annotations.note: `Classical.axiomOfChoice` (and `Classical.choice`) provides AC, foundational to Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chain in a partial order

anchor.snippet: A chain in

annotations.note: `IsChain r s` expresses that `s` is totally ordered by relation `r`.

annotations.label: Zorn's lemma

anchor.snippet: Zorn's lemma states that

annotations.note: `zorn_le` (and `exists_maximal_of_chains_bounded`) is Mathlib's statement of Zorn's lemma.

annotations.label: Every vector space has a basis

anchor.snippet: the proof that every vector space has a basis

annotations.note: `Basis.ofVectorSpace K V` produces a basis of any vector space over a division ring.

annotations.label: Every proper ideal is contained in a maximal ideal

anchor.snippet: the proof that every proper ideal

annotations.note: `Ideal.exists_le_maximal` states every proper ideal sits inside some maximal ideal.

annotations.label: Well-ordering theorem

anchor.snippet: The axiom of choice is equivalent with the fact that a well-order can be defined on every set

annotations.note: `WellOrderingRel` defines a well-order on any type, with `WellOrderingRel.isWellOrder` providing the `IsWellOrder` instance.

annotations.label: Examples of well-ordered sets

anchor.snippet: Simple examples of well-ordered sets are the natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has `IsWellOrder` instances for `ℕ` and ordinals (via `Ordinal`).

annotations.label: Transfinite induction

anchor.snippet: Well-orders allow a generalization of mathematical induction

annotations.note: `Ordinal.induction` and `WellFounded.induction` provide transfinite induction principles.

anchors.snippet: Well-orders allow a generalization of mathematical induction
